title: Chapter 20: The Music Room
content: The fundraiser was a beautiful prison.

Crystal chandeliers threw prisms across the ballroom floor. Julian's hand sat warm at the small of my back, steering me through clusters of Blackwood donors whose smiles never reached their eyes. I'd said the right things, laughed at the right moments, tilted my head at the precise angle that said *grateful* and *humble* and *future Mrs. Linton* all at once. My face ached from it. My ribs ached from it. Everything ached from the performance of being someone I could almost believe I was.
So I ran.

Not obviously — I'd learned that much in three years. I excused myself to the restroom, took a left instead of a right, and slipped through the service corridor that connected the ballroom wing to the music building. My heels clicked against the old marble, each echo a small rebellion. The sounds of the fundraiser — string quartet, clinking glasses, the low hum of money congratulating itself — faded behind me like a fever breaking.

The music room smelled like rosin and dust and old wood. The kind of smell that doesn't lie. I kicked off my heels, and the cold of the hardwood floor seized my feet like a rebuke. Good. I wanted to feel something real.

I sat at the Steinway — the big one, the one that had lived in this room longer than most of the faculty — and lifted the fallboard. The keys were yellowed ivory, slightly chipped at the edges. Honest. I pressed middle C. It rang out pure and slightly flat, the way a note sounds when it hasn't been tuned in a while but still remembers what it's supposed to be.
I played Chopin. Badly. On purpose.

The Nocturne in E-flat, the one I'd learned when I was fifteen and still believed beauty was something you earned. I played the runs sloppy, dragged the tempo, let my fingers stumble into wrong notes and then lean into them like they'd been the plan all along. It sounded like a confession. It sounded like the girl I used to be before I learned to calculate every breath.
The music filled the room — not beautifully, but fully. For two minutes and forty-seven seconds, I was just hands on keys. Just sound. Just a person in a room.
Then the door clicked.
Not the creak of someone walking in. A click. Metal. Deliberate.
I stopped playing. The last note hung in the air, dying, and in its silence I heard the second sound: the turn of a key.
I turned on the bench.

Heath was leaning against the door, one shoulder pressed to the wood, a brass key dangling from his index finger like a pendulum. He was still in his event suit — charcoal, tailored, the kind of thing the Cruz money bought its children when it couldn't buy them warmth. His tie was loosened. One button undone at his throat. He looked like he'd been drinking, but I knew better. Heath Cruz didn't need alcohol to look dangerous. He just needed to be in a room with a locked door.
"You—"

"Locked us in." He said it like he was commenting on the weather. Like trapping me in a room was the most casual thing he'd done all evening. His eyes moved across my face the way a hand moves across a surface it's missed — slowly, deliberately, cataloguing. "You ran."
"I needed air."
"You needed out." He pocketed the key. "There's a difference."

My pulse was doing something it had no business doing. I could feel it in my throat, in my wrists, behind my eyes. The room hadn't been small before he walked in. Now the walls were pulling inward like lungs at the end of a breath.
I stood. He stayed by the door. Twelve feet of hardwood floor between us, and every inch of it charged.

He moved first. Not toward me — along the wall, circling. His hand dragged across the bookshelf, disturbing dust, the pads of his fingers leaving faint lines on the spines of music theory texts no one read anymore. I turned to keep him in my sight, and we were moving in parallel now, two bodies in orbit, looking for the right angle of approach.
"You left the fundraiser," I said. Stupid. Obvious. The kind of small talk you resort to when the real talk is too heavy to lift.
"I left the minute I saw you leave." No hesitation. No shame. He said it the way you say *I breathed* or *my heart beat.* Just a fact. Just his body doing what it was built to do.

He reached the piano. His finger — his index finger, the one I couldn't stop looking at — traced the fallboard in a slow arc. The same finger that had once traced the heart line on my palm during a thunderstorm in third year, both of us squeezed into the space beneath the library stairs while the lights flickered, while the rain hammered the windows, while he'd said *this line means you'll love someone so much it kills you* and I'd said *that's not how palmistry works* and he'd said *who's talking about palmistry?*
The memory hit me like a slap. I wouldn't let it show.
"Did you think of me?"
The question landed in the center of the room like a stone dropped into still water. I felt the ripples move through my chest, my stomach, my knees.
"Every day."
I answered without hesitation because the hesitation would've been the lie. Three years of silence, and I gave him the truth like a gift or a weapon — I couldn't tell which, and I didn't care.
His jaw tightened. I watched the muscle leap beneath the skin, the way it always did when he was fighting something he wanted to say. When he lost that fight.
"Liar." The word was soft. Almost gentle. Which made it worse. "You think of him."
The accusation was a blade. It went in clean. I didn't bleed where he could see.
"I think of both of you. That's the difference between us. I don't pretend otherwise."

"I don't pretend—" He stopped himself. His hand was still on the fallboard, his finger still tracing that slow, maddening arc. The wood gleamed faintly where the oil from his skin marked it. "You're going to marry him."

It wasn't a question. It was an accusation wearing the clothes of one. I could smell him now — cedar and smoke and something underneath that was just *him*, that chemical signature my body had memorized when I was seventeen and never filed away. The radiators were ticking. Heat pushed through the vents, thick and dry, and the room was too warm, and my dress was too tight, and he was too close even at a distance.
"I'm going to do a lot of things," I said. "None of them are your business anymore."
"Anymore." He tasted the word. "That's the part that matters, isn't it? The *anymore.*"

He moved. Not slowly this time. Three deliberate strides and he was at the bench, at the edge of my space, close enough that I could see the fleck of gold in his left iris that only showed in certain light. This light. The same damn light.

I didn't retreat. My body wouldn't let me. My body, the traitor, had decided something three years ago and never consulted my mind. I held my ground. The silk of my dress brushed the leg of his trousers. Static electricity arced between the fabric — I felt it, a tiny shock, a tiny proof that we were still capable of conducting something.
He reached for my hand.

Not gently. Not asking. His fingers closed around my wrist the way they used to — thumb pressing into the pulse point, grip firm but not bruising, the grip that said *I could hold you here forever and I might* — and then he felt it.
The scar.

His face changed. The controlled mask cracked, just for a second, just enough for me to see the thing underneath — need, raw and animal and so vast it frightened me. His thumb found the raised line on my palm. The pact scar. The one we'd given each other with a heated paper clip and a bottle of stolen vodka three years ago, pressing our bleeding palms together like children making a vow we didn't understand. Mine had healed thick and white. A ridge of tissue that caught the light.
He traced it with his thumb. Slowly. The pad of his thumb dragging across the scar the way a person reads Braille — searching for meaning in raised flesh.
"You kept it."

Three syllables. Each one cracked. His grip tightened on my wrist. Not enough to hurt. Enough to *hold.* His face was unreadable again, but his hand was a confession. The scar was a message in a bottle and he was reading it now, three years late, and the words were eating him alive.
I didn't answer. There was nothing to say that the scar hadn't already said for me.
My hand moved before I could stop it.

I grabbed his wrist. Hard. Possessive. My fingers closed around the tendons, felt the pulse hammering beneath his skin — fast, faster than mine, which meant his calm was a lie too. I held him the way he used to hold me. The way no one else had ever held me. The way that said *mine* and *I know you* and *don't you dare pretend this isn't real.*
The touch rewrote three years of silence in a single second.

His breath caught. I felt it through my grip — the arrested expansion of his chest, the way his whole body went rigid like a wire pulled taut. My breath caught too. We were standing there in the blue-and-gold light of the music room, his thumb on my scar, my hand on his wrist, breathing each other's air, and I couldn't remember why I'd ever let go. I couldn't remember why I'd ever walked away. I couldn't remember anything except the pressure of his skin on mine and the way it felt like coming home to a house that was already on fire.
Neither of us could breathe.
Then Julian's voice came through the door.
"Cat? Are you in there? The car is ready."

The sound broke something. Not the moment — the moment was unbreakable, it would live in the walls of this room forever — but the silence that had been holding us both. Julian's voice was warm and patient and slightly concerned, the voice of a man who loved me in the way he understood love, which was thoroughly and completely and without any of the danger that made love worth having.
Neither of us moved.

I was frozen. Cat's hand on Heath's wrist. Heath's thumb on Cat's scar. Julian on the other side of the door. The triangle made physical. A single wall between two worlds — one where I was Catherine Linton-In-Waiting, and one where I was just Cat, still bleeding from a cut she'd never let close. The wall was wood and plaster and decades of Blackwood architecture, and it was the thickest thing in the world.

I could hear Julian's footsteps. The subtle shift of his weight outside the door. The rustle of his coat. He was right there. Ten inches of wall between his hand and Heath's chest, and neither man knew it, and I was the only one who could feel the geometry of it, and it was crushing me.
"Cat?" A little louder now. A little more insistent.
I pulled away.

The break in contact was violent. Not gradual — nothing gradual about it. It was like tearing off a bandage that had fused with the skin beneath. I felt the separation in my nerve endings, in the cold air that rushed into the space where his warmth had been, in the phantom pressure of his thumb that lingered on my scar like a ghost that refused to leave.
I stepped back. One step. Two. The distance between us widened like a wound.

I straightened my dress. Smoothed my hair. Lifted my chin. I could feel it happening — the transformation — the way my spine lengthened and my shoulders squared and my eyes went from *wrecked* to *composed* in the space between two heartbeats. Catherine Linton-In-Waiting, reporting for duty.

Heath watched it happen. I could see him watching — the way you watch a building collapse in slow motion, or a painting burn. His expression wasn't hurt. It was worse than hurt. It was the look of a man recognizing something he'd built being used by someone else. Something between admiration and nausea lived in the set of his mouth.
"You've gotten better at the mask."
His voice was quiet. An observation and an indictment and a eulogy, all in the same breath.
"You've gotten better at the cruelty."

I said it back just as quietly. And I watched the recognition move across his face — not surprise, not defensiveness, just the grim acknowledgment that I'd seen him. That I'd always seen him. That the cruelty was a mask too, and we were both standing here wearing faces we'd spent three years perfecting, and underneath them we were still the same two people who'd pressed bleeding palms together and promised *always* without knowing what it cost.
Both recognize both. That was the worst part.
"Cat?" Julian again. Closer now. His shadow moved beneath the door.

I crossed to Heath. He didn't flinch. I reached into his pocket — his jaw tightened but he let me — and retrieved the key. Our fingers brushed. Even that was too much. I crossed to the door and unlocked it, the mechanism grinding like a confession.
I opened it a fraction. Just enough to show my face. Just enough to hide the room behind me.
"I was just practicing. I'll be right out."

Julian's hand appeared on the door. Warm. Certain. The hand that had never broken me and never would, and somehow that was the problem — it had never broken me, so I'd never had to rebuild, so I'd never had to feel the terrifying exhilaration of surviving someone who could.
"Take your time," he said. "I'll wait."

I heard the service entrance click shut behind me. I didn't turn around. I already knew the room was empty. Heath Cruz, the ghost who'd never stopped haunting me, had evaporated through the back door the way he always did — leaving nothing but the scent of cedar and smoke and the echo of a question I couldn't answer.

I walked out. Julian's arm found mine — warm, steady, certain. I leaned into it because that was what Catherine Linton-In-Waiting did. Because that was the choreography. Because the music had changed and I was dancing to a different tempo now.
My palm was burning.

Not the surface burn of a fresh wound. Something deeper. Something that had been lit three years ago and had never fully gone out, just smoldered under the skin, waiting for oxygen. Heath's thumb had been the oxygen. And now the scar was a coal, glowing, and I couldn't blow it out.

Ten minutes later, in the car, I pressed my hand to my thigh and felt it still burning. Julian's arm around my shoulders, his thumb drawing lazy circles on my upper arm, his voice murmuring something about the fundraiser, about the donors, about the spring gala. I nodded at the right moments. I said *that sounds lovely* and *I'd like that* and all the things that kept the machine running.

In bed that night, Julian asleep beside me with one hand resting on my hip, I pressed my scarred palm flat against my sternum. The skin over my heart was warm too — or maybe that was the scar projecting, sending its heat inward, spreading the fire to the place it would do the most damage.

I could still feel his thumb. I could still feel the exact pressure, the exact trajectory, the way he'd traced the line like he was reading a sentence he'd written himself. I could still feel my own hand closing around his wrist like a vow I hadn't meant to make.
The wall was still there. Ten inches of wood and plaster and three years of choices I couldn't unmake. Julian on one side. Heath on the other. And me, pressed flat against it, burning on both sides.
My palm wouldn't stop burning.
